entries.statement: Knowledge of model interpretability and explainability methods, including SHAP values, LIME, partial dependence plots, and when explanation requirements should influence model selection.

Knowledge of AI governance structures, including model risk management, AI lifecycle oversight, accountability assignment, and the organizational conditions that enable responsible AI deployment at scale.
Knowledge of AI risk taxonomy, including model risk, algorithmic bias, opacity, adversarial vulnerability, data poisoning, training data integrity failures, and systemic AI failure modes.
Knowledge of responsible AI principles and their operationalization, including fairness metrics, explainability methods, human oversight requirements, and AI impact assessment methodologies.

Knowledge of AI regulatory frameworks, including the EU AI Act risk classification structure, conformity assessment requirements, prohibited use categories, and obligations on providers versus deployers.
Knowledge of AI governance framework components, including AI system registries, tiered review processes, impact assessment methodologies, and post-deployment monitoring requirements.
Knowledge of responsible AI principles and audit methods, including fairness metric selection, bias test design, explainability documentation standards, and human oversight protocols.
Knowledge of AI regulatory compliance requirements, including EU AI Act conformity assessment obligations, prohibited use categories, and documentation requirements for high-risk AI systems.

Knowledge of AI literacy concepts, including what AI is and is not, how ML models work at a conceptual level, AI limitations and failure modes, and responsible AI principles for non-technical audiences.
Knowledge of AI risk categories, including performance risk, bias and fairness risk, explainability risk, robustness risk, safety risk, privacy risk, and regulatory compliance risk.

Knowledge of algorithmic fairness concepts, including group fairness metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds, calibration), individual fairness, and the mathematical trade-offs between competing fairness definitions.
Knowledge of AI explainability methods, including SHAP, LIME, counterfactual explanations, attention visualization, and the appropriate application of each method type by model class.
Knowledge of AI regulatory requirements, including the EU AI Act prohibited use categories, high-risk system obligations, conformity assessment requirements, and technical documentation standards.

Knowledge of AI and machine learning-specific model risk considerations, including data drift, concept drift, black-box explainability requirements, and how traditional model risk frameworks must adapt for non-parametric, ensemble, and foundation models.
